## Local Gravity and Observer Bookkeeping

The previous section built the stationary field. This section turns to the local question: how is that field read by matter and observers?

That question matters because the field variables themselves are not yet the whole physical story. A scalar well or a shift field becomes physically meaningful only when the framework can say how local clocking, transport, and packaged magnitudes are altered in its presence. The point of observer bookkeeping is therefore not to add extra ornament to the field theory. It is to connect the structured stationary condition to local physical reading without blurring together what is contextual, what is transported, and what remains globally conserved.

The local observer is not reading gravity from a distant source in one step. It is reading the structured stage at its own location. A clock samples that structure through its cycle-carrying fermic scale. A moving particle samples it through transported momentum. A rotating or directional background samples it through the shift side of the bookkeeping. The variables in this section are the minimal grammar for those local readings.

### Local contextualization of the fermic scale

The first local quantity is the context-dressed fermic scale

$$
p_{f,c}(x) = p_f G(x).
$$

Here $G(x) = \sqrt{1 - 2\theta_0(x)}$ is the scalar clock factor introduced in the previous section. This relation states the central gravity-side reading of the stationary background: the local fermic scale is dressed by context.

That is the cleanest M1 expression of gravitational clock slowing in the stationary regime. The field does not first act by changing an external time parameter. It changes the local cycle-carrying scale through which clocks are read.

### Mechanical transport in a structured stage

The second local quantity is the transport momentum in the presence of shift structure,

$$
\Pi_i = p_i - p_{f,c}A_i.
$$

This expression is simple but important. It tells us that the local mechanical momentum is not just the bare momentum variable $p_i$. It is the momentum read against a structured local stage that already carries directional information through $A_i$.

The role of $\Pi_i$ is to keep transport gravity-first. The shift structure is not an afterthought. It changes the locally relevant transport bookkeeping directly.

This is where directional gravity becomes local. In a rotating stationary situation, for example, the observer does not merely add a later correction to a scalar clock factor. The local transport frame is already structured by $A_i$. Frame-drag-like behavior belongs here because it is a readout of directional deformation, not a decorative GR comparison added after the fact.

### Local magnitude versus stationary generator

Once the context-dressed fermic scale and the local transport momentum are in hand, the natural packaged local magnitude is

$$
M_{g,loc} = \sqrt{p_{f,c}^2 + \gamma^{ij}\Pi_i\Pi_j}.
$$

This quantity is useful because it summarizes the local gravitationally dressed state of the particle or observer in one place. But it should not be confused with the stationary transport generator itself.

That distinction matters. A locally packaged magnitude such as $M_{g,loc}$ tells us how the local state is being read. A stationary generator such as $H_{g,stat}$ tells us how stationary transport is organized. Those are related objects, but they are not interchangeable. If the distinction is dropped, the bookkeeping quickly becomes muddy.

The practical rule is simple. Use $M_{g,loc}$ when the question is about local contextualized magnitude. Use $H_{g,stat}$ when the question is about stationary transport and conserved generator structure.

The supporting convention sheet for this local observer bookkeeping is collected in Appendix 3.5A.

### Local versus asymptotic reading

Gravity also forces a distinction between local and asymptotic quantities.

A field can look weak in one regime and still impose a meaningful local clock dressing nearby. A rotational shift structure can look small at infinity and still matter decisively for local transport. That is why the gravity chapter insists on observer bookkeeping rather than speaking only in global terms.

The local readout belongs to the background actually experienced by the observer. The asymptotic readout belongs to the bookkeeping standard imposed far away. Both can be useful, but they answer different questions.

### Local clocking rule

The local clocking lesson is that the scalar field dresses the clock-carrying fermic scale before motion is read. Motion-side slowing is then computed relative to the locally relevant stationary frame, not relative to a distant asymptotic standard.

The detailed comparison with the familiar stationary proper-time formula, including Kerr-style dragged-frame language, is collected in the stationary correspondence section.

### What local readout establishes

The main local lesson of the stationary gravity program is therefore compact.

The scalar well dresses the local fermic scale through $p_{f,c} = p_f G$. The shift structure modifies transport through $\Pi_i = p_i - p_{f,c}A_i$. Local packaged magnitude is summarized by $M_{g,loc}$, but should not be identified with the stationary transport generator. And clocking depends on the local stationary frame, not only on asymptotic comparison standards.

That is enough for the mainline observer layer. The next section collects the classical stationary correspondence checks before the gravity chapter moves on to the nonstationary question: what survives, and what changes, once the field itself becomes time dependent.
